metadata.title: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back

beats.narration_text: The phase dial argument being half the rotation angle is the piece that clicked for me, but I want to push on observability. You said squaring the wavefunction hides the minus sign, so the 720-degree weirdness feels untestable. Convince me otherwise: (1) describe an interference setup where I rotate only one path by 360 degrees and the minus one actually shows up; (2) show me the exact phase factors on the top and bottom components at 180 degrees; (3) explain whether a spin-one particle would need one turn or two.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: The phase dial argument being half the rotation angle is the piece that clicked for me, but I want to push on observability. You said squaring the wavefunction hides the minus sign, so the 720-degree weirdness feels untestable. Convince me otherwise: (1) describe an interference setup where I rotate only one path by 360 degrees and the minus one actually shows up; (2) show me the exact phase factors on the top and bottom components at 180 degrees; (3) explain whether a spin-one particle would need one turn or two.

beats.narration_text: Rotate a baseball all the way around and it comes back as itself. Rotate an electron's state all the way around — and it comes back as the negative of itself. Only a second full turn restores it completely.

card.copy:
Why an Electron Needs
Two Full Turns to Come Back

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Rotate a baseball all the way around and it comes back as itself. Rotate an electron's state all the way around — and it comes back as the negative of itself. O

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical vector rotating 360 degrees and returning to its starting direction — contrast with a spinor that picks up a minus sign"

beats.narration_text: A classical vector makes a full turn and its direction resets to exactly where it started. That is what 360° means for an ordinary object. Quantum spin is different.

A qubit state is written with the angle theta over two. So a 360° turn of the physical spin corresponds to only 180° in the state — which flips its sign. The minus sign is real. Neutron interferometry has measured it.

card.copy:
Rotate an electron 360 degrees.
The state picks up a minus sign.
How can a full turn not return you to the start?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A qubit state is written with the angle theta over two. So a 360° turn of the physical spin corresponds to only 180° in the state — which flips its sign. The mi

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Bloch sphere with theta/2 parameterization — show that a full 2π rotation of the Bloch vector corresponds to only 180 degrees in state space"

beats.narration_text: The Bloch sphere makes this concrete. A qubit state uses the angle theta over two, not theta. The north pole is theta equals zero. The south pole is theta equals pi. A full trip around the sphere — theta goes from zero to two-pi — only covers half the range of the Bloch vector.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: spinor rotation 360 degrees showing the state picking up a minus sign — probability density unchanged, amplitude sign flipped"

beats.narration_text: After one full 360° turn: the Bloch arrow returned to its starting point, but the state picked up a factor of minus one. The observable — spin-up probability — is unchanged, because we square the amplitude. The minus sign hides in the amplitude.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: second 360-degree rotation completing 720 degrees total — show the minus-one-squared restoring the original spinor state"

beats.narration_text: After a second full 360° turn — total 720° — the state picks up minus one squared, which equals plus one. The state truly returns to its original self. 720° is the spinor period.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: neutron interferometry experiment showing the two beam paths acquiring a relative phase — demonstrating the 720-degree spinor period experimentally"

beats.narration_text: Is the minus sign measurable? Not by measuring spin directly — minus-one-squared is plus one. But in an interference experiment, a beam split into two paths where one beam's spin is rotated 360° will cancel with the other beam — because the amplitudes add, not the probabilities.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: SU(2) vs SO(3) comparison — show the double cover relationship where two spinor states map to one rotation matrix"

beats.narration_text: Neutron interferometry confirmed it in 1975. A neutron beam split at a crystal. One branch passed through a magnetic field that rotated its spin by a controlled angle. At 360°, the interference fringes shifted. At 720°, they reset. The experiment reads out the spinor period directly.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: spinor identity summary — 360 degrees gives minus sign, 720 degrees returns to original state, with the Bloch sphere completing both rotations side by side"

beats.narration_text: A classical vector and a quantum spinor both live in three-dimensional space — but the spinor has twice as many distinct configurations. Two-to-one maps from the spinor space to the sphere; mathematicians call it the double cover of the rotation group.
A classical rotation repeats at 360°. A spinor repeats at 720°. The minus sign is hidden from direct measurement but visible in interference. Neutrons confirmed it in the lab.

card.copy:
Classical rotation: period 360°.
Spinor: period 720°.
Minus sign hides in amplitudes.
Interference makes it visible.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A classical rotation repeats at 360°. A spinor repeats at 720°. The minus sign is hidden from direct measurement but visible in interference. Neutrons confirmed

beats.narration_text: Your electron is still spinning. Paste this into Claude and prove the 720-degree identity from the matrices.

props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
props.command: claude "explain spinors in 2D: show how a spin-1/2 particle's state transforms under rotation, and demonstrate the 720-degree identity with explicit matrix multiplication"
